coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of three-dimensional porous reduced graphene oxide hydrogel/carbon dots for high-performance supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Hange
Xie, Pei
Xue, Shaolin
Li, Lingwei
Hou, Xin
Liu, Zhiyuan
Wu, Dajun
Wang, Lianwei
Chu, Paul K.
coredata.dc:description: A composite composed of reduced graphene oxide hydrogel/carbon dots (rGH/CDs) is prepared hydrothermally. The composite has a three-dimensional (3D) interconnected network structure and exhibits good electrical conductivity and mechanical robustness, making it ideal electrode materials in supercapacitors. The carbon dots (CDs) in the reduced graphene oxide hydrogel promotes electron transport and reduces the internal resistance and charge transfer resistance in addition to providing a large surface area. The flexible solid-state supercapacitor comprising the 130μm thick rGH/CDs electrode delivers excellent performance including high gravimetric specific capacitance of 264Fg−1 (up to 301Fg−1 for a 40μm thick electrode), areal specific capacitance of 394mFcm−2 (up to 432Fcm−2 for a 200μm thick electrode), excellent cycling stability (9.1% deterioration after 5000cycles), larger energy density (35.3Whkg−1), as well as high power density (516Wkg−1). This study demonstrates the tremendous potential of rGH/CDs in high-performance flexible energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
CDs
Flexible electronics
Hydrothermal method
Supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the rGH/CDs composite.
(a) Photograph of rGH/CDs and (b) Photograph of the mechanically strong rGH/CDs supporting a weight.
(a–c) SEM images of the GO, rGH, rGH/CDs interior microstructures; (d) TEM image of the CDs; (e–f) TEM images of the rGH/CDs (reaction temperature of 150°C) interior microstructures.
XPS spectra of (a) GO and (b) rGH, C 1s XPS spectra of (c) GO and (d) rGH.

(a) UV–vis absorbance and PL emission (λex =355nm) spectra acquired from the aqueous CDs solution; (b) Fluorescence decay curves of the CDs, rGH/CDs dispersed in DMF; (c) Raman scattering spectra obtained from the CDs, rGH, and rGH/CDs; (d) FTIR spectra of the CDs, rGH, and rGH/CDs.

(a) CV of the rGH/CDs, rGH, and GO supercapacitors at a scanning rate of 50mVs−1; (b) Galvanostatic charging/discharging curves of the rGH/CDs, rGH, and GO supercapacitors at a current density of 5Ag−1; (c) Nyquist plots of the rGH/CDs, rGH, and GO electrode; (d) Cycling stability of the rGH/CDs, rGH, GO supercapacitors at a current density of 5Ag−1.

(a) CV and (b) Galvanostatic charging/discharging curves of the flexible solid-state device; (c) Nyquist plots of the rGH/CDs electrode; (d) Thickness dependence of areal capacitance and mass capacitance of the rGH/CDs film at a current density of 1Ag−1.

(a) Leakage current and (b) self-discharge curves of the rGH/CDs supercapacitor; (c) CV curves of the device at 50mVs−1 for different bending angles; (d) Cycling performance of the device at 0° and 150°; (e) Ragone plots (power density vs energy density) comparing the rGH/CDs supercapacitors to previously reported solid-state devices made of carbon nanotubes and graphene thin films.

(a) Photograph of the assembled device (top) and schematic diagram (bottom); (b) Photograph of red LED lit by the three supercapacitors in series. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Synthesis of three-dimensional porous reduced graphene oxide hydrogel/carbon dots for high-performance supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices, Ministry of Education, and Department of Electronic Engineering, East China Normal University, 500 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200241, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Department of Materials Science and Engineering, City University of Hong Kong, Tat Chee Avenue, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A composite composed of reduced graphene oxide hydrogel/carbon dots (rGH/CDs) is prepared hydrothermally. The composite has a three-dimensional (3D) interconnected network structure and exhibits good electrical conductivity and mechanical robustness, making it ideal electrode materials in supercapacitors. The carbon dots (CDs) in the reduced graphene oxide hydrogel promotes electron transport and reduces the internal resistance and charge transfer resistance in addition to providing a large surface area. The flexible solid-state supercapacitor comprising the 130μm thick rGH/CDs electrode delivers excellent performance including high gravimetric specific capacitance of 264Fg−1 (up to 301Fg−1 for a 40μm thick electrode), areal specific capacitance of 394mFcm−2 (up to 432Fcm−2 for a 200μm thick electrode), excellent cycling stability (9.1% deterioration after 5000cycles), larger energy density (35.3Whkg−1), as well as high power density (516Wkg−1). This study demonstrates the tremendous potential of rGH/CDs in high-performance flexible energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Flexible solid-state supercapacitors, to satisfy the increasing energy storage demand by portable and flexible electronic products, have attracted a lot of attention [1–6]. Owing to attractive features such as light weight, good flexibility, a large power density and long cycle life, supercapacitors can be used synergistically with conventional batteries [7–16]. Graphene, a unique 2D material suitable for supercapacitors [17], has attracted intense attention due to its excellent mechanical flexibility, electrical conductivity, chemical stability and large specific surface area. In addition, ultrathin graphene films have a small internal resistance and better ion diffusion properties resulting in higher apparent capacitance and rate capability. However, these advantages may not be realized in devices with thick films and large mass loading [8]. For instance, the specific capacitance of supercapacitors composed of ultrathin grapheme films theoretically can reach 550Fg−1 [17], but in most flexible solid-state supercapacitors, the specific capacitance is 80–118Fg−1 at a current density of 1Ag−1 [14–17]. In this respect, macroscopic semiconductor materials with a three-dimensional (3D) porous network structure are attractive electrode materials on account of the good electrical conductivity and mechanical robustness [18,19]. The typical 3D graphene macrostructure such as graphene hydrogels can be prepared by one-step reduction of the graphene dispersion [20–23]. 3D graphene macrostructures possess a large specific surface area (up to 1000m2 g−1 based on methylene blue molecular absorption) so that electrolyte ions can diffuse rapidly through the porous and interconnected network in the unique graphene gel [24,25]. More recently, 3D graphene materials have been applied to all-solid-state supercapacitor and the supercapacitor has excellent specific capacitance (160–240Fg−1), high rate performance, and cycling stability [20–25].

CDs have been shown to have good potential in chemical and biomedical applications because of the low toxicity and biocompatibility [26–35]. They also have good optical properties and are good electron acceptors and electron donors [36,37]. Recently, the conducting capability of CDs has been characterized by Bhattacharjee et al. [38] and a graphene quantum dots (GQDs)//GQDs symmetrical micro-supercapacitor with a high areal specific capacitance (468.1μFcm−2) has been fabricated [30]. Nandi et al. have found that fluorescence from the GQDs in the GQDs-PANI hybrid is quenched drastically and the dc conductivity increases by three orders of magnitude compared to GQD, indicating charge transfer between GQDs and PANI chains [38,39]. Fluorescence quenching in the CDs-GO system has been observed and attributed to ultrafast electron transfer from CDs to GO with a time constant of 400fs [40]. Therefore, it is possible that a supercapacitor based on CDs as well as CDs composites has good electrochemical properties.

The excellent capacitive performance of the supercapacitor with a 3D graphene macrostructure can be prepared with a liquid electrolyte, but it requires a relatively complex packaging process to prevent leakage of the liquid electrolyte and is usually incompatible with flexible applications. In this work, a novel flexible solid-state supercapacitor based on rGH/CDs films is fabricated using H2SO4-polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) gel as the electrolyte. The device has excellent characteristics (for example, 70% retention at 20Ag−1), high gravimetric specific capacitance (264Fg−1 at 1Ag−1), areal specific capacitance of 394mFcm−2, and stability (specific capacitance retention of 90.9% after 5000 charging/discharging cycles at 5Ag−1).

Polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG-200) (CP), glucose (GC) (AR), NaOH (99.7wt%), H2SO4 (98wt%), KMnO4 (99.5wt%), H2O2 (30wt%), HCl (37wt%), sodium nitrate (99.95wt%), PVA (AR), graphite powder (GP), and ascorbic acid (ACA) were used in the experiments and the aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water.

The GO was prepared by the modified Hummer method [41]. Briefly, 2g of natural graphite flake were mixed with 46mL of H2SO4 (98wt%) and 2g of sodium nitrate in a conical flask and stirred for 0.5h in an ice-water bath. Then, 6g of KMnO4 (99.5wt%) were added slowly to the mixture while the temperature was kept at less than 20°C for 1.5h, followed by stirring for 1.5h at 35°C. The solution was diluted with 80mL of deionized water and stirred for 30min at 95°C. 20mL of H2O2 (30wt%) were added slowly and the mixture was filtered and washed 10 times with 0.5molL−1 HCl, followed by removal of the residual sulfate ions with deionized water. Finally, the GO was freeze-dried at −60°C under vacuum conditions.

The rGH samples were prepared by a hydrothermal reduction method. Briefly, 242mg of ascorbic acid (ACA) powder were added to 15mL of 4mg/mL aqueous GO dispersion and sonicated for 2h. The suspension was maintained at 90°C for 2h. Then the rGO suspension was sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 120°C for 12h. Finally, the rGH was freeze-dried at −60°C under vacuum conditions.

The CDs were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method [42]. One g of glucose, 3mL of PEG-200, and 15mg of NaOH were introduced to 35mL of deionized water and stirred for 0.5h. The mixture was sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 150°C for 6h. After cooling to room temperature, the brown solution was filtered through a 0.22μm membrane several times. The solution was dialyzed for 48h to remove residual reactants in deionized water and the CDs were freeze-dried at −60°C under vacuum conditions to yield a brown powder.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the rGH/CDs composite was fabricated hydrothermally. 40mg of CDs were added to 10mL of 4mg/mL aqueous rGO dispersion and sonicated for 2h. The suspension was sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 150°C for 12h. After cooling to room temperature, the sample was taken out with a tweezer for subsequent experiments.

The surface morphology of the samples was examined by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; JEOL JSM-5600LV) and the Raman scattering spectra were acquired on a dispersive Raman spectrometer (Jobin Yvon XploRA) between 0 and 2000cm−1. The optical absorbance was determined by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry (UV–vis; PerkinElmer Lambda 35) and photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed on the Edinburgh FLS 920. In the time-resolved fluorescence measurements, the fluorescence signals were collimated and focused onto the entrance slit of a monochromator with the output plane equipped with a photomultiplier tube (HORIBA Fluoro Maxe-4). All the measurements were carried out at room temperature.

The electrochemical measurements were performed on the CHI660E electrochemical workstation and the specific capacitance was calculated from cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charging/discharging curves and respectively expressed as C 1 and C 2 (Fg−1) as follows: [43–45]
(1)
C 1
=
2
mv
V f
−
V i
∫
V i
V f
idV
(2)
C 2
=
2 i Δt
m Δ U
where m is rGH/CDs mass of one electrode (g), V(V) is working potential with initial potential V
i and final potential V
f
, and v is the potential scan rate, i is the discharge current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), and ΔU is the voltage change (V) during the discharge process. The energy density (E) and power density (P) of one electrode were calculated from the charging/discharging curves using the following equations: [43–45]
(3)
E =
1 2
C Δ
U 2
(4)
P = E / Δ t

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out with an AC voltage with an amplitude of 10mV in the frequency range of 0.01Hz to 100kHz at 1.0V (near the oxidation peak potential). The cycling tests were performed by galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements at a constant current density of 5Ag−1 for 5000cycles. The device was first charged to 1.0V at 2mA and then the potential was retained at 1.0V for 3h while the leakage current data were acquired. In the aqueous electrolyte test, two identical electrodes were separated by a filter paper by clipping and dipped in the 1molL−1 H2SO4–PVA solution.

The rGH/CDs have the form of a mechanically strong hydrogel cylinder with a diameter of 1.3cm and height of 1.8cm as shown in Fig. 2a. The cylinder can support a weight of 100g without noticeable deformation, indicating that rGH/CDs has good mechanical robustness as shown in Fig. 2b.

As shown in Fig. 3a, the GO nanosheets synthesized by the improved Hummer method are not stacked but resemble scattered petals. The rGH is prepared by reducing GO and rGH still contains a small quantity of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface. During the hydrothermal reaction, the nanosheets have a certain stack and so the rGH looks like moss as shown in Fig. 3b. The rGH/CDs are assembled by rGO sheets and CDs via hydrophobic and π-π interactions in 3D space and the rGH/CDs possess an interconnected porous network with a pore size ranging from tens of nanometers to hundreds of nanometers as shown in Fig. 3c. Fig. 3d depicts the TEM images of the CDs together with the distribution histogram, which demonstrates that the CDs have an average size of 2.9nm and standard deviation of 0.5nm. The black CDs in the rGH/CDs composite can be seen clearly from the high-magnification TEM images in Fig. 3e and f. In addition, the rGH/CDs retain the layered morphology probably due to the support of the rGO flakes. The 3D interconnected network of rGH/CDs is expected to accelerate transfer of electrons during charging and discharging and improves the electrochemical performance.

XPS spectra are used to analyze the functionalities and the existence of covalent bonds in the samples in depth. Specifically, Fig. 4a shows the XPS survey spectra of GO, which exhibits two strong peaks that can suggest the presence of C and O. Fig. 4b shows the XPS spectrum of rGH, which reveals the presence of C and O, no other hetero element was detected. At the same time, Fig. 4c displays that the C 1s spectrum of GO contains three different binding energies at 288.1, 286.7, and 284.4eV, which can be attributed to CO, CO, and CC bond, respectively. Compared with CC as depicted in Fig. 4d, the C 1s spectrum of the composite shows a significant decrease in the CO component. This result indicates that the oxygen-containing groups have been removed.

UV–vis absorbance and PL emission spectra acquired from the aqueous CDs solution are displayed in Fig. 5a. There is a narrow peak at 240nm in the UV–vis absorbance spectrum as result of the π → π ∗ transition of the CDs [38]. The peak at 350nm is assigned to the n → π ∗ transition of the functional groups on the CDs [42]. A bright blue peak at around 455nm is observed upon excitation of the aqueous solution of CDs at 355nm. The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs can be calculated by the following formula: [[46].]

ϕ = ϕ
r
×(I/I
r )×(A
r /A)×(n 2/n
r
2),

where φ is the QY, I is the integrated PL emission intensity (excited at 355nm from the CDs and quinine sulfate), n is the refractive index (1.334 for deionized water), A is the absorbance (less than 0.1) of the UV–vis absorbance at 355nm (deionized water), and the subscript “r” refers to the standard. The QY of the CDs is estimated to be 41% when quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1molL−1 of H2SO4 (ϕ
r
=0.54) is selected as the standard control. The CDs are excellent electron donors and electron acceptors according to the luminescence decay curves (excited at 386nm) and they are effectively quenched by either N,N-diethylaniline as electron donors or 2,4-dinitrotoluene as electron acceptors. The results are similar to previous results [37]. The fluorescence decay time of the CDs and rGH/CDs dispersed in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) is calculated to be 6.5 and 1.4ns from the fluorescence decay curves, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5b. The fluorescence decay time of the rGH/CDs is shorter than that of the CDs indicative of electron transfer between the rGH and CDs in the rGH/CDs.

The chemical structure of the samples is characterized by Raman scattering and FTIR (Fig. 5c and d). As shown in Fig. 5c, the CDs, rGH, and rGH/CDs show the characteristic D band at 1351cm−1 and G band at 1592cm−1, corresponding to the well-documented D and G bands of graphite. The D/G band intensity ratio (ID/IG) reflecting the ratio sp3/sp2-hyridized carbon is a measure of the extent of disordered graphite [47]. The ID/IG ration of the CDs is calculated to be 0.45 thus revealing a small density of defects. Electrochemical reduction converts many oxygenated groups on the GO sheets to non‑oxygenated groups and generates different topological defects and vacancies. Hence, both the rGH and rGH/CDs have larger ID/IG ratios (1.34 and 1.16). As shown in Fig. 5d, the typical peaks of CDs appear at 2845cm−1, 1590cm−1, 1423cm−1, 1038cm−1, 835cm−1, indicating that there are COOH and NH2 groups on the surface because glucose and PEG-200 are used as raw materials during synthesis. The typical peaks of rGH appear at 2783cm−1, 1575cm−1, 1392cm−1, 1048cm−1, and 785cm−1 and the FTIR spectra of rGH/CDs show the typical peaks of CDs and rGH.
To evaluate the advantages of the rGH/CDs as electrodes in a supercapacitor, the electrochemical performance of the GO, rGH, rGH/CDs electrodes are evaluated and compared as shown in Fig. 6 .

The specific capacitance of the flexible solid-state supercapacitor is calculated from the CV curves and galvanostatic charging/discharging curves. Fig. 7a depicts the CV curve of the device in the range between 0 and 1V at various scanning rates. Even at a large scanning rate of 200mVs−1, the CV loop exhibits approximately rectangular sharp indicating a low contact resistance and excellent capacitance. Besides, the shape of the galvanostatic current charging/discharging curve is symmetrical and triangular, implying that the supercapacitor has ideal capacitance characteristics as shown in Fig. 7b. The gravimetric specific capacitances of the device calculated from the galvanostatic charging/discharging curves are 264Fg−1 and 210Fg−1 at currents of 1mA and 10mA, respectively. The results indicate that CDs promotes electron transport and reduces the internal resistance and charge transfer resistance in the rGH.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is performed to investigate the capacitive properties and electrode conductivity of the rGH/CDs samples, as shown in Fig. 7c. The experiment data were fitted by an equivalent circuit shown in the inset of Fig. 7c, where the R
s is the equivalent series resistant, R
ct is the charge transfer resistance, Z
w is the Warburg impedance, C 1 is the electric double capacitance. Fig. 7c presents the Nyquist plot in the frequency range between 0.01Hz and 100kHz. The plots are acquired by an arc in the high frequency region and a straight line in the low frequency region. The high frequency loop represents the equivalent series resistance (ESR) which is related to the electrode conductivity and charge transfer resistance in the electrode materials. The straight line in the low frequency region reflects the capacitive properties of the electrode (a vertical line for the ideal capacitor). Fig. 7c discloses a small transfer resistance in the rGH/CDs in the high frequency region. The capacitance performance of the rGH/CDs supercapacitor is good because the slope is approximately a straight line in the low frequency region. In the equivalent circuit the R
ct and R
s value of the 1th (0.82Ω and 1.72Ω) rGH/CDs electrode was much lower than the 1000th (1.12Ω and 2.13Ω). In addition, the R
ct and R
s value of the rGH/CDs (0.82Ω and 1.72Ω) was much lower than the rGH (1.5Ω and 3.32Ω) and GO (2.3 and 4.28Ω) electrode, as shown in Fig. 6c. Hence, the rGH/CDs have small charge-transfer resistance as result of CDs and rGH having excellent conductivity.

The performance of supercapacitor electrodes composed of thin films is usually not comparable to that of thicker supercapacitor electrodes [8]. In this paper, the rGH/CDs films are used to prepare thick electrodes and the thick rGH/CDs electrode has areal capacitance that is one order of magnitude higher than that of supercapacitors reported previously [2]. The capacitive performance of the rGH/CDs films is evaluated as a function of electrode thickness (40–200μm) in the gel (H2SO4-PVA) electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 7d, the rGH/CDs supercapacitor comprising the 130μm thick film electrode has an areal specific capacitance of 394mFcm−2. But the areal capacitance starts to saturate in the gel (H2SO4-PVA) electrolyte when the electrode thickness exceeds 130μm. The maximum areal specific capacitance of 432Fcm−2 is achieved from the 200μm thick film, as shown in Fig. 7d. The specific capacitance decreases slowly with increasing film thickness in the gel (H2SO4-PVA) electrolyte before saturation (130μm), beyond which the specific capacitance diminishes quickly due to limited infiltration of the electrolyte as shown in Fig. 7d. The results are much larger than those of previous carbon materials thin film supercapacitors (5–10μm and 30mFcm−2) [8]. The results further confirm that the rGH/CDs with a conductive network have a large surface area and excellent infiltration of the electrolyte into the network.

In practical applications, to determine the leakage current and self-discharging properties of solid-state devices is of importance. The device is charged to 1.0V at 5mA and maintained at 1.0V for 3h before and the current through the supercapacitor is recorded. The current loss of the supercapacitor, in constant voltage mode, is compensated by the current through the device, while the compensating current can be regarded as a leakage current. In the device, the current quickly stabilizes at 10.2μA and the current which is essentially the leakage current of the device is shown in Fig. 8a. The current of 2.32μA per mg electrode is much smaller than that of the supercapacitor (17.2μA, 5.5μA per mg electrode) fabricated with the carbon nanotube/polyaniline composites, indicating that our device has a small leakage current. As shown in Fig. 8b, the device after being charged to 1.0V for 15min underwent a rapid self-discharge process in the first half hour, which gradually slowed after several hours. Finally the output voltage of the device reached about 0.44V after 25h. These results demonstrate that our device is desirable for practical applications.

In the mechanical bending test, the CV curves show almost the same capacitive behavior at various angles (Fig. 8c), indicating that the excellent flexibility and mechanical robustness. To assess the cycling performance and durability of the device, a constant current charging/discharging test for 5000cycles is conducted at a bending angle of 150° and current density of 5Ag−1. After 5000 charging/discharging cycles, only 9.8% decay is observed as shown in Fig. 8d. The remarkable mechanical flexibility and capacitive performance can be ascribed to the excellent mechanical robustness of the highly interconnected 3D network structure of the rGH/CDs.

To assess the performance of the device, the Ragone plot is presented in Fig. 8e. The graph clearly shows that the device with thicker electrodes and large mass loading has excellent energy density and power density compared to previously reported devices made of graphene hydrogel films [8–14]. This is important to thin film energy storage devices in practice. The device has a power density (516Wkg−1) and energy density (35.3Whkg−1) after normalizing with the electrode entire and they are superior to the supercapacitors based on paper coated with carbon nanotubes (460Wkg−1 and 18.3Whkg−1) and inferior to free-standing graphene thin films with no substrate (1310Wkg−1 and 44.8Whkg−1) [10,15]. Nonetheless, we believe that the performance of the rGH/CDs can be improved by reducing the substrate thickness [7,48].

To assess the practicality of the flexible solid-state supercapacitor composed of the rGH/CDs films, a device is assembled by connecting three symmetrical supercapacitors in series as shown in Fig. 9a. The device is charged to 3.0V at a current density of 10.0Ag−1 and used to power a light-emitting diode (LED, 3mm diameter) as shown in Fig. 9b.

An interconnected 3D rGH/CDs composite electrode fabricated without binders hydrothermally deliver better performance than GO and rGH. The ultra-small CDs have a large specific surface area and provide abundant active sites on the rGH/CDs composites. The conductive CDs boost electron transport and reduces the charge transfer resistance and internal resistance of the electrode materials. The CDs in the electrode also act as an electron acceptor and electron donor. The flexible solid-state supercapacitor composed of 130μm thick rGH/CDs films have a high gravimetric specific capacitance of 264Fg−1 at 1Ag−1, areal specific capacitance of 394mFcm−2, energy density of 35.3Whkg−1, power density of 516Wkg−1, cycling stability (9.1% capacitance decay after 5000 charging/discharging cycles), and mechanical flexibility. The highly interconnected 3D rGH/CDs composite electrode is desirable for high-performance flexible energy storage devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61072003) and City University of Hong Kong Applied Research Grant (ARG) No. 9667122.
